questions.q: In Hindu cosmology, what kind of feature is Mount Meru?
questions.explanation: Meru, also called Sumeru, is described in the Puranas as the golden cosmic mountain at the center of the universe.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, what is Mount Meru most often said to be made of?
questions.explanation: The Puranas describe Meru as a golden mountain, radiant and luminous at the heart of the cosmos.

questions.q: In Puranic geography, at the center of which great continent (dvipa) does Mount Meru stand?
questions.explanation: The Puranas place Meru at the center of Jambudvipa, the innermost of the ring-shaped continents.

questions.q: In Hindu cosmology, what role does Mount Meru serve in relation to the universe?
questions.explanation: Meru is regarded as the axis of the universe, the central pillar around which the cosmos is arranged.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, which celestial bodies are said to revolve around Mount Meru?
questions.explanation: The Puranas describe the sun, moon, and stars as circling around Mount Meru as it stands at the center of the world.

questions.q: Whose abode is traditionally placed at the very summit of Mount Meru?
questions.explanation: Puranic tradition places Brahma's abode at the summit of Mount Meru.

questions.q: In the churning of the ocean of milk (Samudra Manthana), what role was played by the cosmic mountain (named Mandara in the Puranas)?
questions.explanation: In the Samudra Manthana the Puranas name Mount Mandara as the churning-rod, turned by the devas and asuras in the ocean of milk.

questions.q: By what alternative name is Mount Meru also commonly known in the texts?
questions.explanation: Meru is frequently called Sumeru, both names referring to the same golden cosmic mountain.

questions.q: Whose cities and dwellings do the Puranas say are arrayed upon the slopes of Mount Meru?
questions.explanation: The Puranas describe the slopes of Meru as holding the cities of the gods.

questions.q: What is the general nature of Mount Meru in Hindu thought?
options: An ordinary hill in the Himalayas
questions.explanation: Meru is understood as a sacred cosmic mountain forming the spiritual and geographic center of the world.

questions.q: Which class of deities, the guardians of the directions, are said to be arrayed around Mount Meru?
questions.explanation: The Lokapalas or Dikpalas, the guardian deities of the directions, are arrayed around Mount Meru.

questions.q: In the Samudra Manthana, the cosmic mountain used as the churning-rod was supported on the back of which avatar of Vishnu?
questions.explanation: Vishnu took the form of Kurma, the tortoise, to support the churning-mountain on his back.

questions.q: Which serpent served as the churning-rope wound around the cosmic mountain during the churning of the ocean of milk?
questions.explanation: The great serpent Vasuki was used as the rope coiled around the churning-mountain in the Samudra Manthana.

questions.q: Which sacred city of Indra, king of the gods, is associated with the heights of Mount Meru?
questions.explanation: Amaravati, the celestial city of Indra, is associated with the divine realms atop Mount Meru.

questions.q: In Puranic cosmology, Mount Meru is surrounded by a series of concentric ring-shaped continents and what feature between them?
questions.explanation: Puranic geography arranges concentric continents (dvipas) separated by encircling oceans, with Meru at the center.

questions.q: The detailed cosmology describing Mount Meru and Jambudvipa appears most prominently in which body of texts?
questions.explanation: The Puranas, such as the Vishnu Purana and Bhagavata Purana, give the fullest accounts of Meru and the cosmic geography.

questions.q: On the southern side of Mount Meru, within Jambudvipa, lies the region the texts call Bharatavarsha. What does Bharatavarsha represent?
options: The land of the Indian subcontinent
questions.explanation: Bharatavarsha, the land identified with the Indian subcontinent, is placed to the south of Mount Meru within Jambudvipa.

questions.q: Which river is traditionally said to fall from the heavens and divide into branches upon reaching Mount Meru?
questions.explanation: The celestial Ganga is described as descending and dividing into streams upon the slopes of Mount Meru.

questions.q: Mount Meru is frequently described as having golden cities and gardens belonging to the gods, making it conceptually similar to which idea?
questions.explanation: With the abodes and gardens of the gods upon it, Meru is portrayed as a cosmic paradise at the center of the worlds.

questions.q: In Hindu iconography and temple architecture, the towering shikhara or vimana is often understood as a symbolic representation of what?
questions.explanation: Temple towers are often conceived as symbolic representations of Mount Meru, the cosmic mountain and axis of the world.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, the sun's apparent movement of day and night is explained by its circling around which feature?
questions.explanation: Puranic cosmology explains day and night by the sun's circuit around the central Mount Meru.

questions.q: Mount Meru is described as immensely vast and broad. In the texts, its dimensions are given as which of the following?
options: A single peak no larger than a hill
questions.explanation: The Puranas describe Meru as a mountain of vast, near-immeasurable height and breadth befitting the axis of the cosmos.

questions.q: In Puranic cosmology, four great mountains are said to buttress Mount Meru on its four sides. To which group do Mandara and Gandhamadana belong?
options: The guardians of the nether worlds
questions.explanation: Mandara and Gandhamadana are named among the great buttress mountains standing around Mount Meru.

questions.q: Some Puranic accounts describe Mount Meru's four sides as composed of different precious materials. Which combination reflects this tradition?
questions.explanation: Several Puranas describe Meru's four faces as shining with gold, silver, crystal, and lapis (or similar gems), coloring the surrounding regions.

questions.q: In the Bhagavata Purana's cosmology, Jambudvipa surrounding Meru is said to take the shape of what plant feature, after which the continent is named?
questions.explanation: Jambudvipa is named for the great Jambu (rose-apple) tree said to grow near Meru, its fruit giving the region its name.

questions.q: In the churning of the ocean, the churning-mountain Mandara began to sink before Vishnu intervened. In what form did Vishnu steady it?
questions.explanation: When the churning-mountain began to sink, Vishnu assumed the form of Kurma, the tortoise, to bear it upon his back.

questions.q: Puranic texts place a circular range of golden mountains, called the Lokaloka or inner boundary ranges, in relation to Meru. What do these encircling ranges mark?
options: The path of the moon only
The limits of the inhabited worlds
questions.explanation: The encircling ranges, including the Lokaloka, are described as marking the outer limits of the inhabited and illuminated worlds around Meru.

questions.q: The polar concept of Mount Meru is reflected in the term 'Sumeru' being applied in some traditions to which celestial reference point?
options: The setting sun in the west
The cosmic North or pole star region
questions.explanation: Meru is conceived as a polar axis, with the celestial north and the circling of the stars referenced around its summit.

questions.q: In the Ramayana and Puranas, the gods, gandharvas, and apsaras are said to dwell amid the celestial gardens of Meru. Which famed garden of Indra is associated with such divine realms?
questions.explanation: Nandana, the celestial pleasure-garden of Indra, is among the divine gardens associated with the heights of Meru.

questions.q: Puranic cosmology describes Mount Meru as the meeting place where the four cardinal guardians and Brahma's seat unite heaven and earth. This makes Meru a classic example of which religious-symbolic concept?
questions.explanation: As the central pillar joining the realms with Brahma's abode at its summit, Meru embodies the axis mundi, the world-axis of Hindu cosmology.
